description: The most medically significant snake in the Amazon basin and the leading cause of snakebite fatalities in Brazil. Largely terrestrial and nocturnal, inhabiting lowland rainforest, forest edges, and riparian corridors. Highly adaptable and frequently encountered near human settlements and agricultural land.
source_notes: Cunha & Nascimento 1993 · Prudente et al. 2025 (PLOS ONE) · iNaturalist taxon #27923

description: A vivid green arboreal pit viper of Amazonian rainforest canopy and understorey. Primarily nocturnal, coiling on branches 1–4 metres above ground. Despite its arboreal habits, it is responsible for a significant number of snakebite incidents in the region. Its bright green coloration provides exceptional camouflage among foliage.

A robust and secretive pit viper of primary Amazonian forest. Largely terrestrial and nocturnal, sheltering beneath leaf litter and fallen logs. Less frequently encountered than Bothrops atrox but equally dangerous due to potent hemotoxic venom. Confirmed from multiple surveys within the Carajás region.
source_notes: Cunha & Nascimento 1993 · Prudente et al. 2025 · iNaturalist taxon #28012

description: The largest venomous snake in the Americas and the longest pit viper in the world. A strictly nocturnal ambush predator of primary rainforest, relying on exceptional leaf-litter camouflage to ambush small mammals. Highly sensitive to forest disturbance and a strong indicator of intact primary habitat.
source_notes: Cunha & Nascimento 1993 · Campbell & Lamar 2004 · iNaturalist taxon #27921

description: A slender, boldly banded coral snake with red, black, and white rings. Secretive and largely fossorial, spending much of its time beneath leaf litter or in loose soil. Despite its bright warning coloration it is rarely encountered. Bites are uncommon but potentially life-threatening due to potent neurotoxic venom.
source_notes: Cunha & Nascimento 1993 · Prudente et al. 2025 · iNaturalist taxon #30028

description: A semi-aquatic coral snake of Amazonian forest and wetland margins. Distinguished from other regional coral snakes by its distinctive banding pattern and association with wetter habitats. Feeds primarily on elongate prey including other snakes, amphisbaenians, and caecilians.

One of the most widespread and recognisable snakes in the Americas. A powerful constrictor occupying a wide range of habitats from dense rainforest to open savanna and canga. Primarily terrestrial but capable climbers. Feeds on birds and mammals up to the size of small deer.
source_notes: Cunha & Nascimento 1993 · Prudente et al. 2025 · iNaturalist taxon #28216

description: Named for the spectacular iridescent sheen of its scales, which produces a rainbow effect in sunlight due to microscopic ridges acting as prisms. A semi-arboreal constrictor of humid Amazonian forest. Primarily nocturnal, feeding on small rodents, birds, and lizards.

A highly variable arboreal boa found throughout Amazonia, with colour morphs ranging from yellow and orange to grey and brown. Strictly nocturnal, coiling on branches 2–5 metres above ground. An ambush predator of lizards, birds, and small mammals.
source_notes: Cunha & Nascimento 1993 · Prudente et al. 2025 · iNaturalist taxon #28467

description: One of the largest non-venomous snakes in the Neotropics, boldly marked with black and yellow. An agile and fast-moving diurnal predator equally at home on the ground and in the canopy. Feeds on lizards, birds, and small mammals. Highly alert and defensive when approached, inflating its body and striking repeatedly.

An extraordinarily slender, bright green arboreal snake that mimics a vine or grass stem with remarkable fidelity. Diurnal and fast-moving, hunting lizards through active pursuit in the forest understorey. Its rear-fanged venom presents minimal risk to humans. One of the most morphologically distinctive snakes of the region.

A remarkably slender arboreal snake with a disproportionately large head and enormous eyes adapted for nocturnal vision. Moves through vegetation with exceptional agility, bridging gaps between branches. Feeds almost exclusively on sleeping lizards and frogs. One of the most commonly encountered arboreal snakes during night surveys.
source_notes: Cunha & Nascimento 1993 · Prudente et al. 2025 · iNaturalist taxon #30156

description: A semi-aquatic snake strongly associated with slow-moving water bodies, flooded forest margins, and riverside vegetation. Feeds primarily on fish and frogs. Keeled scales and dorsally positioned eyes are adaptations for an aquatic lifestyle. Common along the rivers and wetlands of the Carajás region.

A small ground snake described from specimens collected in the Carajás region and considered endemic to the area. Associated with the open canga (ironstone outcrop) vegetation unique to the Serra dos Carajás plateau. One of the few reptiles considered specific to this habitat type. Very rarely encountered.
source_notes: Cunha et al. 1985 · Dixon 1989 · Cunha & Nascimento 1993 · iNaturalist taxon #89412

description: A slender, fast-moving diurnal snake of Amazonian forest edges and riparian vegetation. Primarily terrestrial but readily climbs into low vegetation. Feeds on frogs, lizards, and small mammals. One of several Chironius species documented from the Carajás region.

A boldly patterned snake that mimics venomous coral snakes, with red, black, and cream banding. Primarily nocturnal and terrestrial, feeding on lizards and small mammals. Its rear-fanged venom presents minimal risk to humans. One of the most commonly misidentified snakes in the region due to its coral snake mimicry.
source_notes: Cunha & Nascimento 1993 · Prudente et al. 2025 · iNaturalist taxon #29955

description: A brilliantly iridescent green arboreal snake with striking metallic scales. Diurnal and fast-moving, hunting frogs and lizards in low vegetation and forest edges. Rear-fanged venom causes minimal symptoms in humans. Commonly encountered in riparian and edge habitats throughout the Carajás region.

A stocky terrestrial snake that mimics the appearance and defensive behaviour of Bothrops pit vipers, flattening its head into a triangular shape when threatened. Feeds primarily on frogs. Despite its alarming defensive display, its rear-fanged venom presents little risk to humans. A remarkable example of Batesian mimicry.

A primitive burrowing snake representing one of the most ancient snake lineages in South America. Boldly banded in red and black to mimic venomous coral snakes. Strictly fossorial, spending almost all of its life underground or beneath leaf litter. Feeds on elongate prey including caecilians and other snakes.

A small, slender snake identified by its distinctive jet-black head contrasting with a reddish-brown body and cream collar. Largely fossorial and nocturnal, feeding on invertebrates — particularly centipedes. Occurs in both primary forest and open canga habitats across the Carajás region.

A robust, stocky terrestrial snake of open habitats including savanna, canga, and grassland margins. Known for a dramatic defensive display in which it inflates its body, hisses loudly, and flattens its head. Despite this display it is entirely harmless. Feeds primarily on toads and is largely immune to toad toxins.
